subject: Statistics
title: Factorial Experiments: Test Everything at Once
description: A 60-second lesson on Factorial Experiments: Test Everything at Once.

hook.text: You want to test two drugs, each at two doses. Test them separately? That's 4 experiments. Or just 1 factorial.

buildup.text: A 2×2 factorial tests all combinations of two factors simultaneously: both drugs, each at high and low dose.

discovery.text: Factorials test main effects and interactions in one experiment. They're more efficient than one-at-a-time designs.

twist.text: The real magic is detecting interactions: drug A works only when drug B is present. You'd miss this testing separately.

climax.text: Fisher championed factorials in the 1930s. They remain the most efficient way to test multiple factors.

punchline.text: Test everything at once. Interactions are the hidden treasure.

quiz.question: What advantage do factorial experiments have over testing one factor at a time?
options: They detect interaction effects between factors that separate experiments would miss
They require fewer participants per group
They eliminate the need for control groups
